questions.question: Derive the average-case time complexity of Sequential Search mathematically, assuming the search key is present and equally likely to be at any position in an array of n elements.
keyPoints: Define average case: sum of comparisons for each possible position divided by n
Divide by n to get average: (n+1)/2
Express in Big-O notation: O(n)
Note: If key not present, exactly n comparisons needed

questions.question: Provide a formal proof of correctness for Sequential Search using loop invariant.
keyPoints: State the loop invariant: After each iteration, all elements from index 0 to i-1 have been examined
Initialization: Before first iteration (i=0), no elements examined - invariant holds
Maintenance: If A[i] ≠ key, increment i; elements 0 to i-1 are now examined - invariant preserved
Termination: Algorithm exits when key found (A[i]=key) or i=n (exhausted)
In both cases, the result is correct - key found or proven absent

questions.question: Explain the worst-case scenario for Brute Force String Matching with an example. Why does this worst-case occur?
keyPoints: Worst-case: pattern 'aaaa...a' and text 'aaaa...aab' or similar
At each shift, pattern almost matches completely before mismatch at last character
Number of shifts: n-m+1, comparisons per shift: m
Total: (n-m+1) × m = O(nm) comparisons
Also occurs with repetitive patterns like 'abcabcabc' searching in 'abcabcabcabcabc'

questions.question: Compare Sequential Search and Brute Force String Matching in terms of their best, average, and worst-case time complexities.
keyPoints: Sequential Search: Best O(1), Average O(n), Worst O(n)
Brute Force String Matching: Best O(n-m+1)=O(n), Average O(n) for large alphabet, Worst O(nm)
Both have O(1) space complexity
Sequential search always makes at most n comparisons
String matching can make nm comparisons in worst case due to nested structure

questions.question: What is the sentinel technique in Sequential Search? How does it improve performance? Analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Add search key at end of array as sentinel to eliminate bounds check
Algorithm: while A[i] ≠ key do i++
Without sentinel: 2 comparisons per iteration (element check + bounds check)
With sentinel: 1 comparison per iteration (only element check)
Worst-case still O(n) but with approximately half the comparisons

questions.question: Derive and prove the worst-case and average-case time complexities for Brute Force String Matching. Include mathematical analysis with summations.
keyPoints: Worst-case: Each of (n-m+1) shifts makes m comparisons: T(n,m) = m(n-m+1) = O(nm)
Best-case: First character never matches: n-m+1 comparisons = O(n)
Average-case: For alphabet size σ, probability of mismatch at each position depends on pattern
Use summation: E[comparisons] = Σ_{s=0}^{n-m} E[comp at shift s]
For random pattern/text over large alphabet: average ≈ n(1 - 1/σ^m)/(1 - 1/σ) ≈ n/σ for large σ
Worst-case patterns (like 'aaa...a') have average-case equal to worst-case

questions.question: Write the complete algorithm for Sequential Search with index tracking and prove its correctness. Include handling of all edge cases.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Input: array A[0...n-1], key k. Output: index i where A[i]=k or -1 if not found
Initialize i = 0
While i < n and A[i] ≠ k: i++
If i < n: return i else return -1
Correctness proof using loop invariant
Edge cases: empty array (n=0), key at first position, key at last position, key not present
Complexity analysis: Best O(1), Worst O(n), Average O(n)

questions.question: Design and analyze a brute force pattern matching algorithm that also counts the number of occurrences of the pattern in the text. What is the time and space complexity?
keyPoints: Algorithm: For each shift s from 0 to n-m, check if P[0...m-1] = T[s...s+m-1]
If match found, increment count
Continue checking all shifts to find all occurrences
Time complexity: In worst case (overlapping matches), still O(nm)
Best case: O(n) when pattern char never matches text
Space: O(1) - only indices and counter
Example: Pattern 'aa' in text 'aaaa' - 3 occurrences found with maximum comparisons
